text: The surgical team performs a right hemicolectomy on an 18-year-old male with a high-grade dysplasia in a 5 cm subsanotic lesion of the right colon, utilizing three trocars. The positioning of the lower 5 mm trocar is adjusted for optimal ergonomics, either aligning with the umbilical incision or placed more cranially based on the patient's abdominal length.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection using a no-touch technique along the tongue fascial plane, preserving the retroperitoneal fascia. The right ureter and gonadal vessels are identified. The ileocolic vessels are ligated at the mesenteric root. The third portion of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas are visualized and separated from the mesentery.

The surgeon delicately dissects the truncal alley, ensuring the anterior superior pancreatic duodenal vein is tight to prevent bleeding during the mesocolon traction maneuver. The right colic muscles are identified and ligated, and the right gas colic is opened to recover the D-plane.

The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal plane caudally and dissects the last jejunal loop. The right paracolic gutter is liberated, and the transverse colon is dissected. The specimen is extracted through the phanacin incision. The ileocolic stump is pulled upwards using a needle, ensuring the posterior wall of the anastomosis is secure before stapling. The stapler is fired, and the anastomosis is checked internally.
The surgeon closes the anatomy with two planes of continuous suture, completing the closure of the surgical site.

The suture of the second plane is knotted to the tail of the first plane. Decision over the surgery is closed to avoid internal hernia. The operation time was one hundred and fourteen minutes without blood loss. The histopathology was PT-PTN-AT-Zero-MX-GT. The drainage was removed in the fifth postoperative day and the discharge was in the fourth postoperative day.
